bibliographic_data.title: Bridging Mechanistic Interpretability and Prompt Engineering with Gradient Ascent for Interpretable Persona Control
bibliographic_data.abstract: Controlling emergent behavioral personas (e.g., sycophancy, hallucination) in Large Language Models (LLMs) is critical for AI safety, yet remains a persistent challenge. Existing solutions face a dilemma: manual prompt engineering is intuitive but unscalable and imprecise, while automatic optimization methods are effective but operate as 'black boxes' with no interpretable connection to model internals. We propose a novel framework that adapts gradient ascent to LLMs, enabling targeted prompt discovery. In specific, we propose two methods, RESGA and SAEGA, that both optimize randomly initialized prompts to achieve better aligned representation with an identified persona direction. We introduce fluent gradient ascent to control the fluency of discovered persona steering prompts. We demonstrate RESGA and SAEGA’s effectiveness across Llama 3.1, Qwen 2.5, and Gemma 3 for steering three different personas, sycophancy, hallucination, and myopic reward. Crucially, on sycophancy, our automatically discovered prompts achieve significant improvement (49.90% compared with 79.24%). By grounding prompt discovery in mechanistically meaningful features, our method offers a new paradigm for controllable and interpretable behavior modification.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a novel framework that adapts gradient ascent to LLMs, enabling targeted prompt discovery for persona control with RESGA and SAEGA methods.
core_content.key_methodology: Two methods, RESGA (RESidual Gradient Ascent) and SAEGA (Sparse Autoencoder Gradient Ascent), are proposed. Both optimize randomly initialized prompts to achieve better aligned representation with an identified persona direction using fluent gradient ascent to control the fluency of discovered persona steering prompts.
outcomes: Effectiveness across Llama 3.1, Qwen 2.5, and Gemma 3 for steering three different personas (sycophancy, hallucination, myopic reward)
Significant improvement on sycophancy with automatically discovered prompts (49.90% compared with 79.24%)
core_content.full_text_markdown: Controlling emergent behavioral personas in Large Language Models is critical for AI safety but remains a challenge. Existing methods face dilemmas: manual prompt engineering is intuitive but unscalable and imprecise, while automatic optimization operates as 'black boxes' without interpretable connections to model internals. This paper proposes RESGA (RESidual Gradient Ascent) and SAEGA (Sparse Autoencoder Gradient Ascent), which optimize prompts for better alignment with persona directions using fluent gradient ascent to control fluency. The methods are demonstrated on Llama 3.1, Qwen 2.5, and Gemma 3 for steering sycophancy, hallucination, and myopic reward personas, achieving significant improvements especially in sycophancy.
